statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
[Level 3 — David Luis Ortega, Where's My Water? ](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3yV8PPnShSw)Let $ n, h $ be positive integers. Swampy the Alligator needs water for his bathtub, and you are tasked with getting it to him. Above his house, there is an $ h \times n $ grid of dirt and water tiles with $ n $ columns numbered $ 1, \ldots, n $ . In column $ i $ , the bottom $ a_i $ tiles are dirt, and the rest on top are water tiles.

To get water to Swampy, you can place a drain on any tile that isn't a dirt tile. Placing a drain takes away all water tiles that can access the drain by moving only down, left, or right on the grid without crossing any dirt tiles.

For example, placing the drain on the $ \times $ in the grid below will drain both the top left and top right water tiles and result in $ 10 $ tiles being drained:

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2207C/60b08f5290a489960693936583d67903134ed07ac9d9f444f8a0730941e52414.png)What is the maximum amount of water you can get to Swampy after placing at most two drains? Note that water tiles that could have been taken by either drain are only counted once.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^3 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line contains two integers $ n $ and $ h $ ( $ 1 \leq n \leq 2000, 1 \leq h \leq 10^9 $ ) — the number of columns and height of the grid.

The next line contains $ n $ integers $ a_i $ ( $ 1 \leq a_i \leq h $ ) — the elevation of the dirt in column $ i $ .

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 2000 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer — the maximum number of water tiles that can be obtained with at most two drains.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
6
7 4
1 3 1 2 3 1 1
8 10
7 5 1 3 2 5 6 8
1 1
1
10 20
5 2 1 2 1 3 6 7 1 1
5 1000000000
1 420420420 1 420420420 1
1 1000000000
1
Output
14
43
0
170
3738738738
999999999

Note
In the first test case, the grid is the example shown in the statement. It is possible to drain $ 14 $ tiles of water by placing the drains as shown:

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2207C/7da283f17711496aeda1750c9fa6966a2045db328c1716b7bf5db31b201bd7c0.png)In the second test case, the grid is shown below:

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2207C/cdb8ceb2594d555a4a03357ca0a3016219c0fde6f88a4974feca510fdd8d2a29.png)It is possible to drain all $ 43 $ tiles of water by placing the drains as shown:

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2207C/076bec40c1db767644d4dd39c5251361a0dba8cfd528cc95ede910ba1d7eb198.png)In the third test case, the grid is a single dirt tile, and no drains may be placed. So, the answer is $ 0 $ .

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: Solve the case of one drain first! You should aim for $$$O(n)$$$.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: If you pick two drains, what's the shape of their overlap?

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2207C - Где моя вода?
Before we begin, note that there are many solution paths to this problem. We include a particularly elegant one here.
First, we attempt to solve the problem for just one drain, located at index $$$1 \leq i \leq n$$$. We notice that a water tile is drained if it has an unobstructed path right and down to the drain, by definition.
Define $$$m_{i,j}$$$ to be $$$\max(a_i, a_{i+1}, \dots, a_j)$$$. Then, a water tile at location $$$(x,y)$$$ is drained if $$$m_{\min(x,i),\max(x,i)} \leq y$$$, that is the maximum elevation between $$$x$$$ and $$$i$$$ is at most $$$y$$$.
Thus, to solve the problem for one drain placed at location $$$i$$$, we can move to the right while keeping a current maximum $$$curr$$$ which is initially $$$a_i$$$, and add $$$h-curr$$$ to our total at each column, and repeat the process to the left. Call this amount of water that a drain can drain at location $$$i$$$ be $$$cnt_i$$$.
Now, let us investigate how we can solve the problem for two drains placed at locations $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$. We can start with the total $$$cnt_i + cnt_j$$$, but we notice that this overcounts water tiles that are drained by both drains. Let us split up the columns into three ranges, $$$[1,i), [i,j], (j,n]$$$. We investigate each range independently.
Consider water tile $$$(x,y)$$$ with $$$x \in [1,i)$$$. Note that by our definition this water tile is drained by both drains if $$$m_{x,i},m_{x,j} \leq y \implies \max(m_{x,i},m_{i,j}) \leq y$$$.
Consider water tile $$$(x,y)$$$ with $$$x \in [i,j]$$$. Note that by our definition this water tile is drained by both drains if $$$m_{i,x},m_{x,j} \leq y \implies m_{i,j} \leq y$$$.
Consider water tile $$$(x,y)$$$ with $$$x \in (j,n]$$$. Note that by our definition this water tile is drained by both drains if $$$m_{i,x},m_{j,x} \leq y \implies \max(m_{i,j},m_{j,x}) \leq y$$$.
From here, we notice that a drain located at elevation $$$m_{i,j}$$$ between $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$ perfectly encompasses this overcount, as per our definition of draining earlier. Thus, if we let $$$k = \arg \max(a_i,a_{i+1},\dots,a_j)$$$, then our answer for drains $$$i,j$$$ is $$$cnt_i + cnt_j - cnt_k$$$.
Thus, to solve the problem, we can compute $$$cnt_i$$$ for each drain $$$i$$$ with the earlier algorithm. Then, we fix drain $$$i$$$ and iterate over all choices $$$j$$$ to the right of $$$i$$$ from left to right, updating our argument max $$$k$$$ if necessary, and then computing $$$cnt_i + cnt_j - cnt_k$$$. Our answer is the maximum over all such values.
Our algorithm is linear for each drain, so computing all values of $$$cnt_i$$$ is $$$O(n^2)$$$. Then iterating over all pairs of drains $$$i,j$$$ is $$$O(n^2)$$$, so this gives us a passing complexity of $$$O(n^2)$$$.
Exercise to the Reader:
By a similar argument, can you show that any maximal draining single drain is in a maximal pair of drains $$$i',j'$$$?
